original_hypothesis.title: SurplusGuildMinter getRewards() Checks Slashed Flag Against Uninitialized Memory, Enabling Permissionless Theft of All Staked CREDIT
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards(), the `slashed` condition is evaluated against the default-initialized (zero) `userStake` return variable BEFORE the actual user stake is loaded from storage. Since `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is 0 in the uninitialized struct, any term where `GuildToken.lastGaugeLoss(term) > 0` will cause `slashed = true` for ALL stakers, regardless of when they staked. This zeroes the user's stake record, permanently locking their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer and orphaning their GUILD gauge weight.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect where reading a return variable before it's populated from storage causes incorrect slash processing. The function is public and callable by anyone for any user, making it a permissionless griefing/theft vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: slashed flag evaluated against default-zero userStake.lastGaugeLoss before storage load at line 228: `userStake = _stakes[user][term]`

preconditions: A lending term has experienced at least one gauge loss (lastGaugeLoss > 0)
At least one user has an active stake in SurplusGuildMinter for that term
No special roles required - function is public

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Line 220: `if (lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss))` where userStake is still default-zero memory
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call getRewards(victim, term) for any victim on any term with historical loss
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker does not profit directly but permanently destroys victim CREDIT stakes and orphans GUILD weight; griefing attack with 100% victim loss

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk across all SurplusGuildMinter stakes on terms with historical losses?
Can the orphaned GUILD weight cause secondary effects on debt ceilings?
Is there any recovery mechanism for the locked termSurplusBuffer CREDIT?
validation_requirements: Deploy test: stake CREDIT on a term, trigger a loss on that term, then call getRewards() and verify slashed=true
Verify that the CREDIT in termSurplusBuffer is irrecoverable after slash
Confirm orphaned GUILD gauge weight persists and affects debt ceiling calculations

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas cost only (~0.001 ETH per victim)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Minimal gas per call to getRewards()
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; 100% loss of victim's staked CREDIT + orphaned GUILD weight
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Pure griefing; infinite ROI for disruption

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after a term has experienced a loss
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systemic: all SGM stakers on affected terms lose funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All SurplusGuildMinter stakers on any term that has had a gauge loss
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all CREDIT staked via SurplusGuildMinter on affected terms

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Any term with lastGaugeLoss > 0 is affected
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace getRewards() line by line confirming uninitialized read

validation_result.mechanism: uninitialized return variable used in slashed check before storage load
validation_result.impact_summary: Any user staking via SurplusGuildMinter on a term that has ever had a gauge loss can be permissionlessly slashed by anyone calling getRewards(). The user's entire stake record is zeroed, their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer is permanently locked, and their GUILD gauge weight is orphaned.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: In SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards(), the return variable `userStake` is declared as `UserStake memory userStake` in the function signature. In Solidity, return variables are default-initialized to zero. The function checks `if (lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss))` BEFORE loading the actual user stake from storage with `userStake = _stakes[user][term]`. Since `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is 0 at the time of the check, the condition simplifies to `if (lastGaugeLoss > 0)`, which is true for any term that has ever experienced a loss. This incorrectly sets `slashed = true` for ALL stakers on that term, regardless of when they staked. The function then zeroes the user's entire stake record and writes it back to storage, permanently destroying their position. Their CREDIT donated to the term surplus buffer is never withdrawn, and the GUILD gauge weight attributed to their position becomes orphaned. The function is public and callable by anyone for any user, making this a permissionless griefing attack.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in the getRewards() function. The return variable `userStake` is default-initialized (all fields zero). The slashed check on the line `if (lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss))` reads from this uninitialized struct. The actual storage load `userStake = _stakes[user][term]` occurs several lines later. This ordering is unambiguously a code defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.line_numbers: function body, around the return variable declarations and first ~15 lines of body
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The `userStake` return variable is default-initialized (all fields zero) per Solidity semantics. The slashed check compares lastGaugeLoss against userStake.lastGaugeLoss (which is 0) BEFORE loading the actual stake from storage. This means any term with lastGaugeLoss > 0 will cause slashed = true for ALL users.

vulnerable_code_snippets.line_numbers: slashed handling block near end of function
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When slashed is incorrectly set to true, the user's entire stake record is zeroed out and written back to storage. The CREDIT in termSurplusBuffer is not withdrawn - it becomes permanently locked.
After getRewards() incorrectly slashes the user and zeroes their stake, unstake() returns immediately without withdrawing the user's CREDIT from the term surplus buffer, making the loss permanent.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls getRewards(victim, term) where term has lastGaugeLoss > 0
8. Slashed block zeros the entire userStake struct
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in the ordering of operations within getRewards(). The slashed flag is computed at step 4 using the default-initialized userStake (step 2), but the actual storage load doesn't happen until step 6. By the time the real userStake is loaded, slashed has already been incorrectly set to true. The function then proceeds to zero out the user's stake record at step 8-9, permanently destroying their position and locking their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would affect this vulnerability. The bug is in the code logic itself, independent of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid and straightforward: (1) A lending term experiences a loss, setting lastGaugeLoss[term] = block.timestamp > 0. (2) User has an active stake in SurplusGuildMinter for that term. (3) Anyone calls getRewards(user, term). (4) The uninitialized userStake.lastGaugeLoss (0) is compared against lastGaugeLoss (> 0), resulting in slashed = true. (5) The user's stake record is zeroed, CREDIT in surplus buffer is locked, GUILD gauge weight is orphaned. No special permissions are required - the function is public.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Every SurplusGuildMinter staker on any term that has experienced a gauge loss (even historically) is vulnerable. An attacker can call getRewards(victim, term) for each victim to permanently destroy their stake. The victim loses 100% of their staked CREDIT (locked in term surplus buffer with no recovery path) and their GUILD gauge weight becomes orphaned. The attack cost is just gas. This is a permissionless griefing attack with total loss for victims.
recommended_mitigations: Move the storage load `userStake = _stakes[user][term]` to BEFORE the slashed check, so that userStake.lastGaugeLoss contains the actual stored value when compared against lastGaugeLoss.

Specifically, reorder the function body so that the first operations are: (1) load userStake from storage, (2) load lastGaugeLoss from GuildToken, (3) compare using the loaded userStake.lastGaugeLoss.
Add an early return if userStake.stakeTime == 0 BEFORE the slashed check, to avoid processing non-existent stakes.
Consider adding a recovery mechanism for incorrectly slashed users if the bug has already been exploited.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full protocol with a lending term. 2. Have userA stake CREDIT via SurplusGuildMinter.stake(term, amount). 3. Create a loan on the term, then trigger a loss via auction/forgive to set lastGaugeLoss > 0. 4. Have userB stake CREDIT via SurplusGuildMinter.stake(term, amount) AFTER the loss (this should be a valid stake). 5. Call getRewards(userB, term) from any address. 6. Verify that userB's stake is zeroed (_stakes[userB][term].credit == 0) despite staking after the loss. 7. Verify that userB cannot recover their CREDIT via unstake().
uncertainties: Whether any stakers have already been affected on a live deployment (if deployed).
Whether the orphaned GUILD gauge weight from slashed users creates secondary issues for debt ceiling calculations.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the CREDIT locked in termSurplusBuffer after incorrect slashing is truly irrecoverable (no admin recovery path other than emergencyAction).
Test the interaction with stake() which also calls getRewards() - confirm that re-staking after incorrect slash also fails or creates inconsistent state.
Verify that orphaned GUILD gauge weight (incrementGauge was called but decrementGauge is never called for slashed users) permanently inflates the gauge weight and affects debt ceiling calculations.
